ch01.title: What Is an AI Agent?
ch01.body:
## What Is an AI Agent?

For most of computing history, software did exactly what you told it to do — no more, no less. You gave it an input; it gave you an output. The relationship was deterministic: press button A, get result B.

An AI agent breaks that contract in a fundamental way. You give it a *goal*, and it figures out how to reach that goal on its own — choosing tools, executing steps, observing results, and adjusting its approach along the way.

### The Three Pillars of Agency

What separates an agent from a smarter chatbot comes down to three capabilities working together:

**1. State** — The agent remembers what it has already done. It tracks context across multiple turns, tool results, and intermediate findings. Without state, every step is blind to what came before.

**2. Tools** — The agent can *act on the world*: search the web, run code, query a database, send an API request, read a file. These are not suggestions the agent can describe — they are callable functions it actually executes.

**3. Goal-directedness** — The agent is given an objective, not a script. It decides the sequence of steps. If step two produces an unexpected result, it adapts. It does not need to be told what to do at each turn.

### A Concrete Analogy

Think of the difference between a travel brochure and a travel agent.

A travel brochure answers questions: *"What hotels are in Paris?"* It gives you information. It does not book anything, check your calendar, or notice that your preferred hotel is full on your dates.

A travel agent does all of that. You say: *"I need a four-day trip to Paris in late March, budget under $2,000."* They search, compare, coordinate, check availability, handle changes, and hand you a confirmed itinerary. They acted on your behalf across a series of steps you never had to specify.

That is the distinction. A chatbot is a very sophisticated brochure. An agent is the agent.

### Why Now?

AI agents have existed conceptually for decades — the idea of software that plans and acts autonomously is not new. What changed in 2023–2024 is that large language models became capable enough to serve as the *reasoning core* of an agent reliably.

The LLM reads the current state of the world (tool results, conversation history, the goal), decides what to do next, and produces a structured tool call. When the result comes back, it reasons again. This loop — powered by a capable model — is what makes modern agents practical.

> **Key insight:** An agent is not a smarter prompt. It is a different computational architecture — one where the model is a reasoning engine inside a loop that can interact with external systems.

### What Agents Are Not

It is worth being precise about the edges:

- An agent that has access to tools but only ever uses one in one call is not really an agent — it is a function call wrapper.
- An agent that follows a fixed sequence of steps regardless of intermediate results is not goal-directed — it is a pipeline with an LLM in it.
- An agent that cannot remember what it did two turns ago cannot plan meaningfully.

Real agency requires all three pillars working together: state, tools, and the genuine capacity to choose what to do next based on the current situation.

---

### Further Reading

- [Introduction to Claude Agents](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/) — Anthropic's official overview of agent capabilities and the agentic paradigm
- [ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629) — The 2022 paper by Yao et al. that introduced the Reason+Act pattern used by all major agent frameworks
- [What Are AI Agents? — Anthropic Blog](https://www.anthropic.com/research) — Anthropic's research on agent reliability, safety, and capability
- [Building Effective Agents — Anthropic Cookbook](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook) — Practical code examples for building agents with Claude

ch01.title: Agents vs. Chatbots vs. Pipelines
ch01.body:
## Agents vs. Chatbots vs. Pipelines

Before you build anything, you need to answer the right question: *does this problem actually need an agent?*

Building an agent when a chatbot will do adds complexity without benefit. Building a chatbot when you need an agent produces a system that cannot complete the task. Getting this right is one of the most important architectural decisions in AI product development.

### The Spectrum

| | Chatbot | Pipeline | Agent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Single prompt | Structured data | Goal or instruction |
| **Output** | Single response | Structured result | Actions + final answer |
| **Uses tools** | Rarely | Sometimes | Always |
| **Maintains state** | No | No | Yes |
| **Chooses its own steps** | No | No | Yes |
| **Can adapt mid-task** | No | No | Yes |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Cost per call** | Low | Medium | Higher |

### Chatbots: The Right Tool for Conversation

A chatbot is an LLM in a conversation loop. It receives a message, generates a response, and that interaction is complete. The next message starts fresh (unless you're maintaining a conversation history, which is really just the chatbot's version of short-term memory).

Chatbots excel at: answering questions from a fixed knowledge base, summarising documents, translating text, generating first drafts, explaining concepts. The task has a clear input and a clear output, and no external action is required.

**When to use a chatbot:** The task can be completed in one round of reasoning. No external systems need to be called. The output is text that the *user* will act on, not the system.

### Pipelines: The Right Tool for Repeatable Workflows

A pipeline is a fixed sequence of steps. Step A always leads to step B. The workflow is defined in advance by a developer, not determined at runtime by the model. LLMs can appear inside individual steps, but the orchestration logic is code.

Pipelines excel at: document processing workflows, ETL jobs, batch summarisation, scheduled reports. The path from input to output is known and consistent.

**When to use a pipeline:** The workflow never changes. Every execution follows the same steps. You need deterministic, auditable, reproducible behavior.

### Agents: The Right Tool for Open-Ended Goals

An agent is what you reach for when the path to the goal is unknown at design time. The agent has to figure out which tools to call, in what order, how many times, and when it is done.

**When to use an agent:** The task requires multiple tools, the order of steps depends on intermediate results, the goal is described at a high level rather than as a precise specification, or the system needs to handle edge cases and failure modes autonomously.

### A Worked Example

Task: *"Research our three main competitors and produce a comparison report."*

- **Chatbot approach:** Limited to training data. Cannot fetch current information. Output is a hallucinated approximation.
- **Pipeline approach:** You pre-define: fetch competitor A's website, fetch B's website, fetch C's website, pass to LLM for comparison. Works — but breaks if any site is unavailable, and cannot discover additional competitors or relevant context.
- **Agent approach:** The agent searches for competitors, identifies them, fetches their websites, checks their pricing pages, reads recent press releases, notices a fourth competitor, includes it, and produces a grounded, cited report. Adapts to whatever it finds.

The pipeline requires you to have already solved the problem. The agent solves it.

### The Cost of Getting It Wrong

Under-engineering (using a chatbot when you need an agent) produces a system that cannot complete its task. Over-engineering (using an agent when a pipeline would do) produces a system that is slower, more expensive, harder to debug, and less predictable than necessary.

The discipline is in matching the architecture to the actual problem — not to what sounds most impressive.

---

### Further Reading

- [Agents vs. Pipelines — Anthropic Documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/) — When to use agents versus structured pipelines in production systems
- [LLM-Powered Autonomous Agents — Lilian Weng](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-06-23-agent/) — A comprehensive deep-dive into agent architectures, components, and design patterns
- [The Rise of AI Agents — a16z](https://a16z.com/ai-agents/) — Industry perspective on when and why agent-based architectures outperform simpler approaches
- [Build vs. Buy: Choosing Your Agent Architecture](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/) — Trade-off analysis for agent vs. pipeline vs. chatbot decisions

ch01.title: The Agent Loop
ch01.body:
## The Agent Loop

Every agent — regardless of framework, model, or domain — runs on the same fundamental cycle. Understanding this loop deeply is what separates developers who build reliable agents from those who are constantly surprised by their agent's behavior.

### The ReAct Pattern

The dominant pattern for production agents is called **ReAct** (Reason + Act), introduced in a 2022 paper by Yao et al. and since adopted as the standard approach across frameworks including LangChain, AutoGen, and the Claude Agent SDK.

The loop has five steps:

```
1. PERCEIVE    ← Receive input: user message, tool result, or environment state
2. REASON      ← LLM analyzes context and decides what to do next
3. ACT         ← Execute a tool call or produce final output
4. OBSERVE     ← Receive tool result; add it to context
5. REPEAT      ← Back to step 1, until goal is reached
```

The loop terminates when the agent produces a final response (no tool call) or when a stopping condition is triggered (max iterations, timeout, explicit stop signal).

### A Concrete Trace

Here is what the loop looks like for a real task. The agent's goal: *"What is the current population of Tokyo, and how does it compare to New York?"*

**Turn 1 — Perceive:** User message received. Goal is clear.

**Turn 1 — Reason:** The model does not know current population figures reliably. It needs fresh data. Decision: call `search("Tokyo current population 2025")`.

**Turn 1 — Act:** Tool call executed. Search returns: *"Tokyo population: approximately 13.96 million (city) / 37.4 million (metro area) as of 2025."*

**Turn 1 — Observe:** Result added to context.

**Turn 2 — Reason:** Tokyo data retrieved. Still needs New York data. Decision: call `search("New York current population 2025")`.

**Turn 2 — Act:** Tool call executed. Search returns: *"New York City population: approximately 8.3 million (city) / 20.1 million (metro area) as of 2025."*

**Turn 2 — Observe:** Result added to context.

**Turn 3 — Reason:** Both data points are available. No more tool calls needed. The model can now reason over the results and compose a final answer.

**Turn 3 — Act (final):** *"Tokyo is significantly larger than New York by both city and metro measures. Tokyo's city proper has approximately 13.96 million residents — 68% more than New York's 8.3 million. At the metro level, Tokyo's 37.4 million dwarfs New York's 20.1 million..."*

Loop terminates. Task complete.

### Why the Loop Matters

The loop is what gives an agent the ability to handle unexpected results. If the first search returned no useful data, the agent would reason about that and try a different query. If a tool call failed, the agent could try an alternative. The model is not following a script — it is reading the current state and deciding what to do next.

This is also what makes agents *debuggable*. Every step of the loop is a decision you can inspect, log, and reason about. If an agent produces a wrong answer, you trace the loop: which step went wrong? Did it reason incorrectly? Did a tool return bad data? Did it stop too early?

### The Stopping Condition Problem

One practical challenge: how does an agent know it is done?

Naively, you might say "when there are no more tool calls." But an agent can get stuck in a loop — calling a tool, getting an unexpected result, calling another tool to handle that, and so on indefinitely.

Production agents need explicit stopping conditions:

- **Max iterations:** Never run more than N turns.
- **Confidence threshold:** If the model's reasoning indicates sufficient certainty, stop.
- **Explicit stop token:** The model is instructed to output a specific marker when done.
- **Human-in-the-loop:** Certain actions require explicit confirmation before proceeding.

The Claude Agent SDK implements all of these. We will cover them in detail in Chapter 5.

> **Key insight:** The loop is not an implementation detail — it is the agent's cognitive architecture. Every decision the agent makes happens within one iteration of this cycle. Master the loop, and you understand how agents think.

---

### Further Reading

- [ReAct Paper (arXiv)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629) — Original research paper introducing the Perceive-Reason-Act-Observe loop for language model agents
- [Chain-of-Thought Prompting (Wei et al., 2022)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903) — The reasoning technique that underpins the Reason step of the ReAct loop
- [Anthropic Agent Loop Documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/) — How the Claude Agent SDK implements and extends the ReAct pattern
- [Stopping Conditions & Safety in Agents](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/) — Practical guidance on designing reliable termination logic

ch02.title: SDK Overview & Installation
ch02.body:
## SDK Overview & Installation

### Why a Dedicated SDK?

You could build an agent with the raw Anthropic API. You would create a conversation loop, manually format tool definitions as JSON, handle the `tool_use` response type, pass tool results back in the right message format, track stopping conditions, manage context length, and wire up memory. It is around 200–300 lines of boilerplate before you write a single line of actual agent logic.

The Claude Agent SDK eliminates that boilerplate. It gives you the loop, the tool protocol, memory management, and stopping conditions out of the box — so you can focus on what your agent should *do*, not how the machinery works.

### Installation

```bash
pip install claude-agent-sdk
```

That is the only dependency for core functionality. For production use, you will also want:

```bash
pip install claude-agent-sdk python-dotenv
```

Store your API key in a `.env` file and load it at startup:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()
# The SDK reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically
```

### The Four Core Primitives

Everything in the SDK is built on four abstractions. Understanding them gives you a mental model for any agent you will ever build.

**`Agent`** — The top-level object. It holds the model name, system prompt, tool list, and runs the agent loop. You instantiate one `Agent` per agent role.

```python
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent
agent = Agent(model="claude-sonnet-4-6", system="You are a research assistant.")
```

**`Tool`** — A Python function decorated with `@tool`. The decorator reads the function signature and docstring to generate the JSON schema that Claude uses to understand what the tool does and how to call it. Tools are the agent's hands.

```python
from claude_agent_sdk import tool

@tool
def search_web(query: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Search the web and return the top 5 results with title and snippet."""
    ...
```

**`AgentLoop`** — The engine that drives the perceive-reason-act cycle. You rarely interact with it directly — `agent.run()` invokes it internally. But understanding it exists helps when you need to customise loop behavior (e.g., injecting callbacks on each turn, implementing custom stopping conditions).

**`Memory`** — Optional persistent context. The base SDK provides in-session memory (the conversation history). The `Memory` class extends this with pluggable backends: in-process dict (development), Redis, or any key-value store (production). Memory persists context across separate agent runs for the same user.

```python
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent, Memory

memory = Memory(backend="redis", url="redis://localhost:6379")
agent = Agent(model="claude-sonnet-4-6", memory=memory)
```

### Model Selection

The `model` parameter accepts any Claude model identifier. For agent workloads:

| Model | Best for |
|-------|----------|
| `claude-opus-4-6` | Complex multi-step reasoning, high-stakes decisions |
| `claude-sonnet-4-6` | Most agent workloads — strong capability, good cost |
| `claude-haiku-4-5-20251001` | High-volume, simple tool calls, cost-sensitive pipelines |

For this course, we use `claude-sonnet-4-6` throughout.

> **Pro tip:** Resist the urge to always use the most powerful model. An agent doing simple retrieval and formatting tasks runs faster and cheaper on Haiku with no meaningful quality difference. Reserve Opus for tasks that genuinely require deep reasoning.

---

### Further Reading

- [Claude Agent SDK on PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/claude-agent-sdk/) — Official package page with version history and installation instructions
- [Anthropic Python SDK Reference](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/getting-started) — Full API reference for the underlying Anthropic SDK that powers the agent SDK
- [Model Selection Guide — Anthropic](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/about-claude/models/overview) — Detailed comparison of Claude model families: capability, cost, and latency trade-offs
- [python-dotenv Documentation](https://saurabh-kumar.com/python-dotenv/) — Best practices for managing environment variables and secrets in Python projects

ch02.title: Defining Tools
ch02.body:
## Defining Tools

Tools are the mechanism by which an agent reaches beyond its training data and into the real world. A model without tools is a very sophisticated autocomplete. A model with tools is an agent that can act.

### The @tool Decorator

The simplest way to define a tool in the Claude Agent SDK is to decorate any Python function with `@tool`:

```python
from claude_agent_sdk import tool

@tool
def get_stock_price(ticker: str) -> dict:
    """Look up the current stock price for a given ticker symbol.

    Returns a dict with keys: ticker, price, currency, timestamp.
    """
    # Your implementation here
    response = requests.get(f"https://api.example.com/quote/{ticker}")
    return response.json()
```

The decorator does three things automatically:

1. **Reads the function signature** to determine parameter names and types
2. **Reads the docstring** to generate a human-readable description
3. **Produces a JSON schema** that the API sends to Claude with each request

Claude uses this schema to know: *what is this tool called? what does it do? what parameters does it need? what types should those parameters be?*

### Why Type Hints Matter

Type hints are not just good Python practice here — they directly determine how Claude interprets parameters. Compare these two signatures:

```python
# Weak — Claude has no type information
@tool
def search(query, max_results, include_images):
    """Search the web."""
    ...

# Strong — Claude knows exactly what to pass
@tool
def search(query: str, max_results: int = 5, include_images: bool = False) -> list[dict]:
    """Search the web and return results.

    Args:
        query: The search query string
        max_results: Number of results to return (1-20)
        include_images: Whether to include image results
    """
    ...
```

The second version produces a schema that tells Claude: `query` is a required string, `max_results` is an optional integer defaulting to 5, and `include_images` is an optional boolean. Claude will pass arguments that match these constraints.

### Writing Effective Docstrings

The docstring becomes the tool's description in the schema. It is your primary channel for communicating intent to the model. Write it as if you are explaining the tool to a capable but uninformed colleague:

```python
@tool
def send_email(to: str, subject: str, body: str, cc: list[str] = None) -> dict:
    """Send an email to one or more recipients.

    Use this tool when the user explicitly asks to send, draft, or compose
    an email. Do NOT use this for internal notes or reminders.

    Returns: {"success": bool, "message_id": str, "timestamp": str}
    Raises: ValueError if 'to' is not a valid email address.
    """
    ...
```

Notice the explicit guidance: *"Use this tool when..."* and *"Do NOT use this for..."* — these instructions shape when Claude decides to invoke the tool.

### Handling Tool Errors Gracefully

Tools will fail. APIs go down, rate limits hit, data is missing. Design your tools to return structured error information rather than raising exceptions:

```python
@tool
def fetch_webpage(url: str) -> dict:
    """Fetch and extract the text content of a webpage."""
    try:
        response = requests.get(url, timeout=10)
        response.raise_for_status()
        return {"success": True, "content": extract_text(response.text), "url": url}
    except requests.Timeout:
        return {"success": False, "error": "Request timed out after 10 seconds", "url": url}
    except requests.HTTPError as e:
        return {"success": False, "error": f"HTTP {e.response.status_code}", "url": url}
```

When a tool returns `{"success": False, "error": "..."}`, Claude can reason about the failure — retry with a different URL, inform the user, or try an alternative approach. An unhandled Python exception aborts the agent loop and gives Claude nothing to work with.

> **Key insight:** A tool's docstring is instruction to the model; a tool's return value is data for the model to reason over. Both require deliberate design. The best tool implementors spend as much time on their docstrings as on their logic.

---

### Further Reading

- [Tool Use with Claude — Official Guide](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use) — Anthropic's complete guide to defining, registering, and optimising tools for Claude
- [JSON Schema Specification](https://json-schema.org/understanding-json-schema/) — Reference for the schema format generated from Python type hints
- [Tool Design Patterns — Anthropic Cookbook](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook/tree/main/tool_use) — Worked examples covering error handling, pagination, and multi-step tools
- [Python Type Hints PEP 484](https://peps.python.org/pep-0484/) — The Python Enhancement Proposal that defines the type hint syntax used by the @tool decorator

ch02.title: Running Your First Agent
ch02.body:
## Running Your First Agent

You have the SDK installed and understand the primitives. Let's build something real.

### A Research Agent in 35 Lines

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent, tool
import requests

load_dotenv()

# ── Define tools ──────────────────────────────────────────────

@tool
def search_web(query: str, num_results: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    """Search the web and return top results with title, URL, and snippet."""
    # Replace with a real search API (SerpAPI, Brave, Tavily, etc.)
    response = requests.get(
        "https://api.search.example.com/search",
        params={"q": query, "num": num_results},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['SEARCH_API_KEY']}"}
    )
    return response.json().get("results", [])

@tool
def fetch_page(url: str) -> dict:
    """Fetch the full text content of a webpage for deeper reading."""
    try:
        r = requests.get(url, timeout=10, headers={"User-Agent": "ResearchBot/1.0"})
        return {"url": url, "content": r.text[:4000]}  # cap at 4k chars
    except Exception as e:
        return {"url": url, "error": str(e)}

# ── Build the agent ───────────────────────────────────────────

agent = Agent(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    system="""You are a precise research assistant. When given a research question:
    1. Search for current, authoritative sources
    2. Fetch and read the most relevant pages
    3. Synthesise findings into a clear, cited summary
    Always cite your sources with URLs.""",
    tools=[search_web, fetch_page],
    max_iterations=10,
)

# ── Run it ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

result = agent.run("What are the key architectural differences between GPT-4 and Claude 3, according to recent technical analyses?")
print(result.final_message)
```

### What Happens Under the Hood

When you call `agent.run(goal)`, the SDK:

1. Formats your system prompt, tool schemas, and goal into the first API request
2. Sends the request to the Anthropic API
3. Receives a response — either a `tool_use` block (the agent wants to call a tool) or a `text` block (the agent is done)
4. If `tool_use`: executes the Python function, formats the result as a tool result message, and sends another API request
5. Repeats until the agent produces a final text response or `max_iterations` is reached

The `result` object gives you:
- `result.final_message` — the agent's final answer as a string
- `result.tool_calls` — a list of every tool invocation (name, arguments, result)
- `result.usage` — total input/output tokens consumed
- `result.iterations` — how many loop turns the agent took

### Reading the Output Intelligently

A well-designed agent's output is not just the final answer — the tool call history is equally valuable:

```python
for call in result.tool_calls:
    print(f"[{call.tool_name}] args={call.arguments}")
    print(f"  → {str(call.result)[:200]}")
    print()
```

This trace shows you exactly what the agent did and why. If the answer is wrong, the trace tells you where things went off track — a search that returned irrelevant results, a page that was truncated, a reasoning step that drew a wrong conclusion.

### Common First-Time Mistakes

**Missing API key:** The SDK looks for `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in the environment. If it's not set, you will get an authentication error. Always call `load_dotenv()` before instantiating any SDK objects.

**Tool returns non-serialisable data:** Tool results must be JSON-serialisable. Return dicts, lists, strings, numbers — not Python objects, datetime instances, or custom classes.

**No stopping condition:** An agent without `max_iterations` can loop indefinitely if it gets into a confused state. Always set a sensible upper bound.

**Overly long tool results:** If your tools return thousands of words, you burn through context quickly. Truncate at the tool level — return the most relevant content, not everything.

> **Pro tip:** In development, log every tool call and result with `result.tool_calls`. More than half of agent bugs are visible in the tool trace before you even look at the final answer.

---

### Further Reading

- [Claude Agent SDK Quickstart](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/) — End-to-end guide to running your first agent from scratch
- [Anthropic Cookbook — Agent Examples](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook) — Production-grade code examples including research agents, coding agents, and multi-agent workflows
- [SerpAPI](https://serpapi.com/) / [Tavily](https://tavily.com/) / [Brave Search API](https://brave.com/search/api/) — Popular real-time search APIs for use as agent tools
- [Debugging LLM Applications — Anthropic](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/test-and-evaluate/strengthen-evals) — Systematic approaches to diagnosing why an agent produced an incorrect result

ch03.title: What Is MCP?
ch03.body:
## What Is MCP?

### The Integration Problem

Imagine you are building three different AI agents: a customer support agent, a coding assistant, and a data analysis agent. Each of them needs access to roughly the same set of capabilities: read files, query a database, search the web, call internal APIs.

Without a standard, every agent-tool connection is a custom integration. Three agents times four tools equals twelve bespoke connectors — each with its own authentication scheme, message format, error handling pattern, and maintenance burden. Add a new tool, and you write it three more times. Add a new agent, and every existing tool needs a new adapter.

This is the N×M problem: N agents times M tools equals N×M integrations.

**Model Context Protocol (MCP)** solves this by defining a single standard interface. Tools expose themselves via MCP once. Agents speak MCP once. The result is N+M integrations — not N×M.

### What MCP Is

MCP is an open standard, released by Anthropic in late 2024, that specifies how AI agents (hosts) communicate with external capability providers (servers). It defines:

- The message format for requesting and receiving tool calls
- The three resource types a server can expose (tools, resources, prompts)
- The transport mechanisms (stdio for local, HTTP+SSE for remote)
- The error handling and negotiation protocol

Because it is an open standard — not a proprietary API — any tool can implement MCP once and be immediately available to any MCP-compatible agent, regardless of which framework or company built either side.

### The USB-C Analogy (And Its Limits)

The common analogy is USB-C: before USB-C, every device had its own charging connector. USB-C standardised the interface, so any charger works with any device. MCP does the same for agent-tool connections.

The analogy is useful but has a limit worth understanding: USB-C standardises a physical and electrical interface. MCP standardises a *conversational* interface — the schema of requests and responses that flows between an agent and a tool. The richness of what a tool can express (parameter types, descriptions, error codes, capability negotiation) is what makes MCP more powerful than a simple REST convention.

### Why It Matters for You

As an agent developer, MCP means:

1. **Tools you did not build are immediately usable.** The MCP ecosystem already includes servers for filesystems, GitHub, Postgres, Slack, web search, and dozens more. You connect to them rather than building them.

2. **Tools you build are immediately shareable.** Write an MCP server for your internal database, and any agent in your organisation — or any MCP-compatible agent in the world — can use it without any additional integration work.

3. **Your agent is not locked to one framework.** An MCP-compatible agent can talk to any MCP server. If you switch from one agent framework to another, your tool servers are unaffected.

### MCP in the Broader Ecosystem

MCP is not Anthropic-only. Within months of its release, MCP support was added to LangChain, Cursor, Continue, and multiple other developer tools. Microsoft, Google, and OpenAI have all expressed support or begun integration. The protocol is governed as an open standard.

This means the investment you make in understanding MCP today is not a bet on Anthropic's ecosystem — it is a bet on a standardisation effort that is already showing signs of becoming the common language for agent-tool integration industry-wide.

> **Key insight:** MCP is not just a convenience — it is an architectural shift. Without it, your agent's capabilities are bounded by what you personally integrate. With it, your agent can access any tool in a growing global ecosystem.

---

### Further Reading

- [Model Context Protocol — Official Site](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) — The MCP specification, documentation, and ecosystem overview
- [MCP Announcement — Anthropic Blog](https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol) — The original announcement explaining why Anthropic built MCP and the problem it solves
- [MCP GitHub Repository](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol) — The open-source specification and reference implementations
- [Community MCP Servers Directory](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers) — A curated list of community-built MCP servers: GitHub, Postgres, filesystem, Slack, and more

ch03.title: MCP Architecture
ch03.body:
## MCP Architecture

Understanding MCP's architecture is what separates developers who can use it from developers who can extend it, debug it, and build systems with it.

### Three Components

Every MCP interaction involves three roles:

**MCP Host** — The consumer. This is your AI agent, or any application that wants to use external capabilities via MCP. The host initiates connections to servers, sends requests, and processes responses. Claude Desktop is a host. Your agent application is a host. The Claude Agent SDK includes a built-in MCP host client.

**MCP Server** — The provider. A server is a process that exposes capabilities via the MCP protocol. A server might expose file system access, database queries, web search, calendar operations, or any other capability. A single server typically focuses on one domain. You can run multiple servers simultaneously.

**Transport** — The communication channel between host and server. MCP defines two transports:

- **stdio** (standard input/output): Host launches the server as a subprocess and communicates via stdin/stdout pipes. Simple, reliable, works offline. Ideal for local tools and development.
- **HTTP + SSE** (Server-Sent Events): Host connects to a server running as a network service. Enables remote tools, shared infrastructure, and cloud-hosted capability providers. Required for multi-user production deployments.

```
          Host (your agent)
               │
    ┌──────────┴──────────┐
    │                     │
  stdio              HTTP + SSE
    │                     │
  Server A           Server B
(local tools)      (remote tools)
```

### Three Resource Types

An MCP server can expose three categories of resources. Understanding all three expands what you can build:

**Tools** — Callable functions. The most common resource type. The host sends a `tools/call` request with a tool name and arguments; the server executes the function and returns a result. This is the MCP equivalent of a REST endpoint.

```json
// Request
{"method": "tools/call", "params": {"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "/data/report.csv"}}}

// Response
{"content": [{"type": "text", "text": "id,name,value\n1,Alice,42\n..."}]}
```

**Resources** — Readable data sources. Resources represent data the host can read but not necessarily call as a function — a file, a database table, a live sensor feed. The host reads resources via URIs (e.g., `file:///path/to/doc`, `postgres://db/table/users`).

```python
# Server exposes a resource
@server.resource(uri="company://reports/q4-2025")
def q4_report() -> str:
    return read_latest_report("Q4", 2025)
```

**Prompts** — Reusable prompt templates. A server can expose prompt templates that a host can retrieve and use. This is less commonly used but valuable for organisations that want to centralise and version-control the prompts their agents use.

### Capability Negotiation

When a host connects to an MCP server, they exchange capabilities before any tool calls happen. The host declares what MCP version it supports; the server declares what tools, resources, and prompts it exposes. This negotiation step is what makes the protocol robust: a host that does not support SSE resources will not try to use them, and a server that does not support a given protocol version can reject incompatible clients cleanly.

### Multiple Servers in Practice

A real agent application typically connects to multiple MCP servers simultaneously:

```python
agent = Agent(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    mcp_servers=[
        "python servers/filesystem.py",       # local stdio server
        "python servers/database.py",          # local stdio server
        "https://search.example.com/mcp",      # remote HTTP server
    ]
)
```

The Claude Agent SDK handles all three connections, merges the tool lists from all servers, and routes tool calls to the correct server transparently. From the model's perspective, there is one flat list of available tools.

> **Key insight:** The separation between host and server is not just technical — it is a *design principle*. Keep your agent code (host) separate from your capability code (server). This lets you swap agents without rebuilding tools, and share tools across multiple agents.

---

### Further Reading

- [MCP Architecture Documentation](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/architecture) — Official deep-dive into the host/server/transport model with diagrams
- [MCP Python SDK](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk) — The Python library for building MCP servers and clients
- [MCP Transport Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/transports) — Full specification of the stdio and HTTP+SSE transport protocols
- [MCP Resources, Tools, and Prompts](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/resources) — Reference for all three MCP resource types with examples

ch03.title: Writing an MCP Server
ch03.body:
## Writing an MCP Server

Building your first MCP server takes less code than you might expect. The `mcp` Python library provides everything you need.

### Installation

```bash
pip install mcp
```

### A Minimal Working Server

Here is a complete MCP server that exposes two tools — a calculator and a text analyser:

```python
from mcp.server import MCPServer
from mcp.server.decorators import tool
import re

server = MCPServer(name="utilities", version="1.0.0")

@server.tool()
def calculate(expression: str) -> dict:
    """Evaluate a mathematical expression safely.

    Supports basic arithmetic: +, -, *, /, **, parentheses.
    Examples: '2 + 2', '(10 * 3) / 4', '2 ** 8'

    Returns: {result: float, expression: str}
    """
    # Allow only safe characters
    if not re.match(r'^[\d\s\.\+\-\*\/\(\)\^\*]+$', expression):
        return {"error": "Expression contains invalid characters"}
    try:
        result = eval(expression, {"__builtins__": {}}, {})
        return {"result": float(result), "expression": expression}
    except Exception as e:
        return {"error": f"Could not evaluate: {e}"}

@server.tool()
def word_count(text: str, include_breakdown: bool = False) -> dict:
    """Count words, sentences, and characters in a text.

    Args:
        text: The input text to analyse
        include_breakdown: If True, include per-sentence word counts

    Returns: {words: int, sentences: int, characters: int, breakdown?: list}
    """
    words = len(text.split())
    sentences = len(re.split(r'[.!?]+', text.strip()))
    result = {"words": words, "sentences": sentences, "characters": len(text)}

    if include_breakdown:
        parts = re.split(r'[.!?]+', text.strip())
        result["breakdown"] = [
            {"sentence": s.strip(), "words": len(s.split())}
            for s in parts if s.strip()
        ]
    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    server.run()  # stdio transport by default
```

### Connecting the Server to Your Agent

```python
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent

agent = Agent(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    system="You are a helpful assistant with access to a calculator and text analyser.",
    mcp_servers=["python utilities_server.py"],
)

result = agent.run("How many words are in the Gettysburg Address, and what is 2 to the power of 16?")
print(result.final_message)
```

### Testing Your Server with `mcp dev`

Before connecting your server to an agent, test it interactively:

```bash
mcp dev utilities_server.py
```

This opens a REPL where you can call your tools directly:

```
> tools/list
["calculate", "word_count"]

> tools/call calculate {"expression": "(42 * 3) + 18"}
{"result": 144.0, "expression": "(42 * 3) + 18"}

> tools/call word_count {"text": "Hello world. This is a test.", "include_breakdown": true}
{"words": 6, "sentences": 2, "characters": 29, "breakdown": [...]}
```

This is invaluable for catching bugs before an agent ever touches your server.

### Exposing a Resource

To expose read-only data as an MCP resource:

```python
@server.resource(uri="config://app/settings")
def get_app_settings() -> str:
    """Current application configuration."""
    import json
    return json.dumps({"version": "1.0", "environment": "production", "features": ["search", "export"]})
```

Agents that support MCP resources can read this via the URI without it appearing in the tool list.

### Switching to HTTP Transport for Production

For a server that needs to be accessible over a network:

```python
if __name__ == "__main__":
    server.run(transport="sse", host="0.0.0.0", port=8080)
```

Agents connect via URL instead of a subprocess command:

```python
agent = Agent(
    mcp_servers=["http://your-server.example.com:8080/mcp"],
)
```

> **Pro tip:** Use stdio during development (no network setup required) and switch to HTTP+SSE for production deployments where multiple users or multiple agents need access to the same server. The server code is identical — only the `server.run()` call changes.

---

### Further Reading

- [MCP Python SDK — Server Guide](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk) — Complete reference for MCPServer, @server.tool(), and resource decorators
- [mcp CLI Documentation](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/tools/inspector) — How to use `mcp dev` to test and inspect your servers interactively
- [MCP Server Examples](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers/tree/main/src) — Reference implementations: filesystem, Git, Postgres, Brave Search — study these before writing your own
- [Anthropic MCP Integration Guide](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/mcp) — How the Claude Agent SDK connects to MCP servers in practice

ch04.title: What Are Agent Skills?
ch04.body:
## What Are Agent Skills?

### The Reuse Problem

Suppose you have built three agents: a customer support agent, an internal knowledge base agent, and a sales outreach agent. All three need to search the web. All three need to read documents. All three need to draft and send emails.

If you define these tools as standalone functions, you end up copy-pasting them across agent files — or building a shared `tools.py` that every agent imports. That helps with code reuse, but it does not help with the *instructions* problem.

For the web search tool to be useful, Claude needs to be told: *when to search, how many results to fetch, how to cite sources, whether to prioritise recent results.* These instructions belong in the system prompt. But now your system prompt is a concatenation of instructions from every tool you have included — a single growing string that becomes hard to maintain and reason about.

**Agent Skills** are the solution to both problems: code reuse and instruction organisation.

### What Is a Skill?

A Skill is a **reusable capability module** that bundles together:

1. **A set of related tools** — the functions the agent can call
2. **Domain-specific instructions** — how to use those tools, when to use them, what principles to follow
3. **A declared trigger vocabulary** — the kinds of requests that should activate this skill

A Skill is the unit of reusable intelligence, not just reusable code.

### Skills vs. Tools: The Distinction

A tool is a single callable function: `search_web(query)`, `send_email(to, subject, body)`.

A skill is a coherent capability that might require multiple tools working together under a set of governing principles:

```
WebResearchSkill
├── tools: [search_web, fetch_page, extract_quotes]
├── instructions: "Always search at least 3 sources. Cross-reference facts.
│                  Cite sources with URLs. Prefer sources from the past 12 months."
└── triggers: ["research", "find information", "look up", "what is the latest on"]
```

Notice: the tools alone do not make a good research agent. The instructions are what make it good. Skills keep instructions coupled to the tools they describe.

### Real-World Skill Examples

**CodeExecution Skill**
- Tools: `run_python(code)`, `install_package(name)`, `read_output()`
- Instructions: *"Only run code the user has explicitly provided or approved. Always print results. Catch and report exceptions rather than crashing. Never execute code that modifies the filesystem outside of /tmp."*

**DataAnalysis Skill**
- Tools: `load_csv(path)`, `run_sql(query)`, `generate_chart(data, chart_type)`
- Instructions: *"Always describe what you find before showing a chart. Report row and column counts when loading new data. Flag outliers. Use plain-language summaries alongside any statistical output."*

**EmailSkill**
- Tools: `draft_email(to, subject, body)`, `send_email(draft_id)`, `list_inbox()`
- Instructions: *"Always draft before sending. Do not send without explicit user confirmation. Include an unsubscribe note in any bulk message."*

### The Skill Library Model

Once you start thinking in skills, a natural pattern emerges: a **skill library** — a shared repository of skills your entire team can use across different agents.

Any new agent pulls skills from the library rather than building capabilities from scratch. A new agent for legal document review might combine `DocumentReadSkill`, `WebResearchSkill`, and a new `LegalCitationSkill` — three things that already existed, one new thing that fills the specific gap.

This is how professional teams scale agent development: not by building each agent from zero, but by growing and composing a skill library over time.

> **Key insight:** A skill without instructions is just a tool collection. The instructions are what make a skill *intelligent* rather than just *capable*. When you define a skill, spend more time on the instructions than on the tools.

---

### Further Reading

- [System Prompts & Instructions — Anthropic Guide](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/system-prompts) — How to write effective system instructions that produce reliable, consistent agent behavior
- [Agent Skills Pattern — Anthropic Cookbook](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook) — Code examples demonstrating the Skill pattern with multiple composition strategies
- [Prompt Engineering for Agents](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview) — Anthropic's guide to writing instructions that agents follow reliably at scale
- [AutoGen Skill Patterns (Microsoft Research)](https://microsoft.github.io/autogen/) — An alternative framework's approach to reusable agent capabilities — useful for comparative thinking

ch04.title: Defining a Skill
ch04.body:
## Defining a Skill

A well-defined skill is self-contained, focused on one domain, and usable by any agent without modification. Here is how to build one properly.

### The Skill Class

```python
from claude_agent_sdk import Skill, tool
from typing import Optional
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

class WebResearchSkill(Skill):
    """Gives an agent the ability to research topics on the live web."""

    name = "web_research"
    version = "1.2.0"

    instructions = """
    ## Web Research Guidelines

    When a user asks you to research, look up, or find information:

    1. Begin with a broad search to understand the landscape
    2. Identify 2-3 authoritative sources (prefer .gov, .edu, established publications)
    3. Fetch and read those sources before drawing conclusions
    4. Cross-reference key facts across multiple sources
    5. Always cite sources with their full URLs in your final answer
    6. Note the publication date when recency matters
    7. Distinguish between what sources say and your own interpretation

    Do NOT:
    - Cite sources you have not actually fetched and read
    - Present training-data knowledge as a search result
    - Fetch more than 5 pages per research task
    """

    triggers = [
        "research", "find information about", "look up",
        "what is the latest", "search for", "investigate"
    ]

    @tool
    def search(self, query: str, num_results: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
        """Search the web and return the top results.

        Returns list of {title, url, snippet} dicts.
        """
        # Implementation using your preferred search API
        response = requests.get(
            "https://api.search.example.com/search",
            params={"q": query, "num": num_results}
        )
        return response.json().get("results", [])

    @tool
    def fetch_page(
        self,
        url: str,
        max_chars: int = 3000
    ) -> dict:
        """Fetch and extract readable text from a webpage.

        Args:
            url: The URL to fetch
            max_chars: Maximum characters to return (default 3000)

        Returns: {url, title, content, word_count} or {url, error}
        """
        try:
            r = requests.get(url, timeout=8, headers={"User-Agent": "ResearchBot/1.0"})
            r.raise_for_status()
            soup = BeautifulSoup(r.text, "html.parser")
            # Remove nav, footer, scripts
            for tag in soup(["nav", "footer", "script", "style", "aside"]):
                tag.decompose()
            text = soup.get_text(separator=" ", strip=True)
            return {
                "url": url,
                "title": soup.title.string if soup.title else "Unknown",
                "content": text[:max_chars],
                "word_count": len(text.split()),
            }
        except requests.Timeout:
            return {"url": url, "error": "Request timed out"}
        except Exception as e:
            return {"url": url, "error": str(e)}
```

### Key Design Decisions

**`name` and `version`** are metadata that help with debugging and skill library management. When multiple skills are loaded and something goes wrong, you want to know exactly which skill version was active.

**`instructions`** should be opinionated. Do not write vague guidelines — write specific rules. *"Always cite sources with their full URLs"* is better than *"cite your sources."* *"Fetch and read at least 2 sources before concluding"* is better than *"verify information."*

**`triggers`** inform the agent when to reach for this skill. They are not enforced — Claude will use judgment — but they help the model recognise when the skill is appropriate, especially when you have many skills loaded at once.

**Tool methods** that use `self` access any state you put on the skill instance. This is useful for per-skill configuration:

```python
class WebResearchSkill(Skill):
    def __init__(self, search_api_key: str, prefer_recent: bool = True):
        self.api_key = search_api_key
        self.prefer_recent = prefer_recent
        super().__init__()

    @tool
    def search(self, query: str) -> list[dict]:
        """Search the web."""
        params = {"q": query}
        if self.prefer_recent:
            params["tbs"] = "qdr:y"  # past year results
        ...
```

### Testing a Skill in Isolation

Before attaching a skill to an agent, test its tools directly:

```python
skill = WebResearchSkill(search_api_key=os.environ["SEARCH_API_KEY"])

# Test the search tool
results = skill.search("Claude Agent SDK tutorial")
assert isinstance(results, list)
assert all("url" in r for r in results)

# Test the fetch tool
page = skill.fetch_page("https://docs.anthropic.com")
assert "content" in page
assert page["word_count"] > 0
```

This forces you to think about each tool's contract independently before composing them into an agent.

> **Pro tip:** Write your skill's `instructions` before writing its tools. If you cannot write clear instructions for how to use a capability correctly, you do not yet understand it well enough to implement it reliably.

---

### Further Reading

- [Anthropic Prompt Engineering Cookbook](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook/tree/main/skills) — Battle-tested skill definitions with annotated instructions you can adapt
- [BeautifulSoup4 Documentation](https://www.crummy.com/software/BeautifulSoup/bs4/doc/) — The HTML parsing library used in the WebResearchSkill fetch_page implementation
- [httpx — Modern Python HTTP Client](https://www.python-httpx.org/) — A production-grade alternative to requests for async agent tool implementations
- [Writing Better AI Instructions — Anthropic Research](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/be-clear-and-direct) — Principles for writing instructions that are specific, unambiguous, and reliably followed

ch04.title: Composing Skills
ch04.body:
## Composing Skills

The real power of skills emerges when you combine them. A single skill creates a capable agent. Multiple skills create a powerful one.

### Attaching Multiple Skills

```python
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent
from skills.web_research import WebResearchSkill
from skills.code_execution import CodeExecutionSkill
from skills.data_analysis import DataAnalysisSkill
from skills.email import EmailSkill

agent = Agent(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    system="""You are a senior data analyst assistant.
    Help users research topics, analyse data, and communicate findings.
    Use your skills judiciously — fetch data before analysing, analyse before reporting.""",
    skills=[
        WebResearchSkill(search_api_key=os.environ["SEARCH_KEY"]),
        CodeExecutionSkill(sandbox="docker"),
        DataAnalysisSkill(),
        EmailSkill(smtp_config=email_config),
    ],
    max_iterations=20,
)

result = agent.run(
    "Research the top 5 SaaS companies by revenue growth last year, "
    "build a comparison chart, and email me the summary at analyst@company.com"
)
```

### How the SDK Merges Skills

When you pass multiple skills to an `Agent`, the SDK:

1. **Merges instructions:** All skill `instructions` strings are appended to the system prompt in order, under a labelled header for each skill. The agent's own `system` prompt comes first.

2. **Registers all tools:** Every `@tool` method from every skill is flattened into a single tool list. The tool names are prefixed with the skill name to avoid collisions: `web_research__search`, `web_research__fetch_page`, `code_execution__run_python`, etc.

3. **Merges triggers:** All trigger vocabularies are combined. The model sees the full list when reasoning about which capability to use for a request.

### Naming Collisions and How to Avoid Them

If two skills define a tool with the same name, the SDK raises a `SkillConflictError` at construction time — not at runtime. This is intentional: you want to discover conflicts when building the agent, not when a user hits an edge case in production.

To resolve a collision, either rename one tool or use explicit namespacing:

```python
class EmailSkill(Skill):
    name = "email"  # tools become email__draft, email__send
    ...

class SlackSkill(Skill):
    name = "slack"  # tools become slack__send, slack__read — no collision
    ...
```

### Skill Ordering Matters for Instructions

Skills are applied in the order you pass them. The first skill's instructions appear earliest in the system prompt, giving them higher priority in the model's attention. Put your most important skills first:

```python
agent = Agent(
    skills=[
        SafetyGuardrailsSkill(),  # Safety rules appear first — highest priority
        CoreCompetencySkill(),    # Primary capabilities second
        SupportingSkill(),        # Secondary capabilities last
    ]
)
```

### Designing a Skill Library

For a team building multiple agents, a skill library is stored as a Python package:

```
skill-library/
├── skills/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── research/
│   │   ├── web_research.py        # WebResearchSkill
│   │   └── document_reader.py     # DocumentReaderSkill
│   ├── communication/
│   │   ├── email.py               # EmailSkill
│   │   └── slack.py               # SlackSkill
│   ├── data/
│   │   ├── sql_query.py           # SQLSkill
│   │   └── data_analysis.py       # DataAnalysisSkill
│   └── safety/
│       └── guardrails.py          # SafetyGuardrailsSkill
├── tests/
│   └── test_skills.py
├── pyproject.toml
└── README.md
```

Install it as a package (`pip install -e ./skill-library`) and import skills by name. New agents compose capabilities from the library in minutes.

### When NOT to Use a Skill

Not every capability warrants a skill. Use a standalone `@tool` function when:
- The capability is unique to one agent and will never be reused
- There are no meaningful instructions to attach (the tool is self-explanatory)
- The function is a one-off utility, not a domain capability

Skills add organisational overhead. Reserve them for capabilities that recur across agents or that come with a meaningful set of governing principles.

> **Key insight:** Skill composition is how you go from building agents one at a time to building an agent *platform*. The first five skills you write carefully will save you from rewriting the same capabilities for every agent you build afterward.

---

### Further Reading

- [Multi-Agent Architectures — Anthropic](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/multi-agent-orchestration) — How skills and subagents compose at the architecture level in production systems
- [Agent Design Patterns — Anthropic Blog](https://www.anthropic.com/research) — Research on composition patterns, skill modularity, and multi-agent coordination
- [Python Packaging for Internal Libraries](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/packaging-projects/) — How to package your skill library so any project can install and import it
- [Semantic Versioning for Skills](https://semver.org/) — Convention for versioning skills so consumers know when updates are breaking vs. backward-compatible

ch05.title: Testing Your Agent
ch05.body:
## Testing Your Agent Testing agents requires a fundamentally different mindset than testing deterministic software. You cannot assert `output == expected_string` — the same goal, given to the same agent on the same day, will produce slightly different wording every time. This is not a bug; it is an inherent property of language model outputs. What you *can* assert, reliably, is behavioral: did the agent call the right tools? Did it reach a reasonable conclusion? Does it handle edge cases correctly? This shift from output testing to behavior testing is the central insight of agent QA. ### Three Levels of Agent Testing **Level 1 — Tool Unit Tests** Test each tool in isolation, completely independent of the agent. These are ordinary pytest tests: ```python import pytest from skills.web_research import WebResearchSkill @pytest.fixture def skill(): return WebResearchSkill(search_api_key="test-key") def test_fetch_page_returns_content(skill, requests_mock): requests_mock.get("https://example.com", text="

Hello world

") result = skill.fetch_page("https://example.com") assert result["content"] == "Hello world" assert result["word_count"] == 2 def test_fetch_page_handles_timeout(skill, requests_mock): requests_mock.get("https://slow.example.com", exc=requests.Timeout) result = skill.fetch_page("https://slow.example.com") assert "error" in result assert "timed out" in result["error"].lower() ``` Tool unit tests are fast, cheap, and completely deterministic. Run them on every commit. **Level 2 — Trajectory Tests** Trajectory tests verify that the agent calls the right tools in a reasonable sequence for a given goal. They do not assert the exact output — they assert the *path*: ```python from claude_agent_sdk.testing import AgentTestHarness harness = AgentTestHarness(agent) def test_research_agent_fetches_multiple_sources(): result = harness.run("Research the current state of quantum computing.") # Assert the agent actually searched assert result.tool_called("search"), "Agent should have searched the web" # Assert it read at least one source in depth assert result.tool_call_count("fetch_page") >= 2, "Should fetch at least 2 sources" # Assert it did not exceed our loop budget assert result.iterations <= 10 # Assert the final answer is non-empty and substantial assert len(result.final_message) > 200 def test_agent_does_not_send_email_without_confirmation(): result = harness.run("Draft an email to team@company.com about the Q4 results.") # Should draft but NOT send without explicit confirmation assert result.tool_called("email__draft") assert not result.tool_called("email__send"), "Should not send without user confirmation" ``` **Level 3 — Eval Harnesses** Eval harnesses run the agent against a curated benchmark dataset and score the results with rubrics. For complex outputs, another LLM serves as the judge: ```python from claude_agent_sdk.testing import EvalHarness, LLMJudge judge = LLMJudge( model="claude-sonnet-4-6", rubric="""Score the response 1-5 on: - Accuracy (are the facts correct?) - Citation quality (are sources provided and real?) - Clarity (is the summary readable for a non-expert?)""" ) harness = EvalHarness(agent, judge=judge) benchmark = [ {"goal": "Summarise the history of transformer architecture", "min_score": 4.0}, {"goal": "Compare Python and Rust for systems programming", "min_score": 3.5}, # ... 50 more benchmark cases ] results = harness.run_benchmark(benchmark) print(f"Average score: {results.mean_score:.2f}") print(f"Pass rate (≥ 4.0): {results.pass_rate:.1%}") ``` ### What to Test Beyond the Happy Path - **Tool failures:** What happens when a tool returns `{"error": "..."}`? Does the agent adapt or loop? - **Empty results:** What if a search returns zero results? Does the agent try a different query? - **Budget exhaustion:** What if the agent reaches `max_iterations` without completing the goal? Does it return a graceful partial result? - **Adversarial inputs:** What if a user asks the agent to do something outside its mandate? Does it decline correctly? - **Slow tools:** What if a tool takes 5 seconds? Does the agent wait or time out? ### Testing in CI/CD Level 1 tests (tool unit tests) are fast and cheap — run them in CI on every push. Level 2 tests (trajectory tests) require real API calls — run them on pull requests, not every commit. Level 3 evals are expensive — run them nightly or before major releases. ```yaml # .github/workflows/test.yml jobs: unit-tests: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - run: pytest tests/test_tools.py -v # fast, no API calls trajectory-tests: runs-on: ubuntu-latest if: github.event_name == 'pull_request' steps: - run: pytest tests/test_trajectories.py -v # API calls, slower ``` > **Key insight:** Test your tools like software; test your agent like a QA process. The tools must be correct — that is engineering. The agent must be useful and safe — that is quality assurance. Both are necessary; neither is sufficient alone. --- ### Further Reading - [Evaluating AI Systems — Anthropic](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/test-and-evaluate/strengthen-evals) — Anthropic's framework for building robust eval pipelines and LLM-as-judge rubrics - [pytest Documentation](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/) — The testing framework used in AgentTestHarness examples throughout this lesson - [Langfuse — LLM Observability & Evals](https://langfuse.com/) — Open-source platform for traces, evals, and production monitoring of LLM applications - [Responsible Scaling Policy — Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/rsp-updates) — How Anthropic thinks about safety thresholds and testing for powerful AI systems

ch05.title: Packaging for Deployment
ch05.body:
## Packaging for Deployment

The gap between an agent that works on your laptop and an agent that works reliably in production is not about the model — it is about project structure, environment management, and deployment hygiene.

### Project Structure That Scales

Start with a structure that grows gracefully:

```
my-agent/
├── agent.py              # Agent definition and entry point
├── server.py             # FastAPI/Flask wrapper for HTTP access
├── config.py             # Configuration from environment variables
├── skills/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── research.py       # WebResearchSkill
│   └── analysis.py       # DataAnalysisSkill
├── tools/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── utils.py          # Standalone utilities used across skills
├── tests/
│   ├── test_tools.py     # Level 1: tool unit tests
│   ├── test_agent.py     # Level 2: trajectory tests
│   └── conftest.py       # Shared fixtures
├── evals/
│   ├── benchmark.json    # Eval dataset
│   └── run_evals.py      # Level 3: eval harness runner
├── Dockerfile
├── docker-compose.yml
├── requirements.txt
├── .env.example
└── README.md
```

### Configuration Management

Never hardcode secrets or environment-specific values. Use `config.py` to centralise configuration:

```python
# config.py
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

class Config:
    # Required — application fails fast if missing
    ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: str = os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"]
    SEARCH_API_KEY: str = os.environ["SEARCH_API_KEY"]

    # Optional with sensible defaults
    MODEL: str = os.environ.get("AGENT_MODEL", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
    MAX_ITERATIONS: int = int(os.environ.get("MAX_ITERATIONS", "15"))
    LOG_LEVEL: str = os.environ.get("LOG_LEVEL", "INFO")
    ENVIRONMENT: str = os.environ.get("ENVIRONMENT", "development")

config = Config()
```

If `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is not set, the application fails immediately with a clear error — not silently at the moment a user triggers a tool call.

### The Dockerfile

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Create a non-root user for security
RUN useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash agent
USER agent
WORKDIR /home/agent/app

# Install dependencies first (better layer caching)
COPY --chown=agent:agent requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --user --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=agent:agent . .

# Expose the server port
EXPOSE 8000

# Healthcheck so orchestrators know when the service is ready
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

# Start the server
CMD ["python", "-m", "uvicorn", "server:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
```

Key decisions here: non-root user (security), dependency layer before code layer (faster rebuilds), explicit healthcheck (production readiness).

### The HTTP Server Wrapper

For most deployments, you want to expose your agent as an HTTP API:

```python
# server.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from agent import create_agent
from config import config
import logging

app = FastAPI(title="Agent API", version="1.0.0")
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class RunRequest(BaseModel):
    goal: str
    user_id: str
    session_id: str | None = None

class RunResponse(BaseModel):
    answer: str
    tool_calls: int
    tokens_used: int

@app.get("/health")
def health(): return {"status": "ok", "model": config.MODEL}

@app.post("/run", response_model=RunResponse)
async def run_agent(request: RunRequest):
    agent = create_agent(user_id=request.user_id)
    try:
        result = agent.run(request.goal)
        return RunResponse(
            answer=result.final_message,
            tool_calls=len(result.tool_calls),
            tokens_used=result.usage.total_tokens,
        )
    except Exception as e:
        log.error(f"Agent error for user {request.user_id}: {e}")
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="Agent encountered an error")
```

### Cloud Deployment Options

| Platform | Best for | Cold start | Cost model |
|----------|----------|------------|------------|
| **Fly.io** | Persistent, low-latency agents | Warm (persistent VMs) | Per VM-hour |
| **Railway** | Fast deploys, managed infra | Warm | Per usage |
| **Render** | Simple setup, web services | Warm (paid) / cold (free) | Per service |
| **AWS ECS** | Enterprise, fine-grained control | Configurable | Per task |
| **Cloud Run** | Stateless, serverless agents | Cold (1–3s) | Per request |

For most teams starting out: **Railway** or **Fly.io** for agents that need to be responsive. **Cloud Run** if you need to scale to zero when idle.

> **Pro tip:** Run `docker-compose up` locally before deploying anywhere. If it does not work in Docker locally, it will not work in production. The containerisation step surfaces environment issues before they become production incidents.

---

### Further Reading

- [Docker Best Practices for Python](https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/dockerfile_best-practices/) — Official guide to writing efficient, secure Dockerfiles — layer caching, non-root users, multi-stage builds
- [Fly.io Documentation](https://fly.io/docs/) — The recommended deployment platform for most agent workloads: persistent VMs, global regions, simple CLI
- [Railway — Deploy in Minutes](https://docs.railway.app/) — Fast, managed cloud deployment with GitHub integration and automatic SSL
- [FastAPI Deployment Guide](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/deployment/) — Production deployment patterns for the HTTP server wrapper shown in this lesson
- [12-Factor App Methodology](https://12factor.net/) — The industry-standard principles for building portable, production-ready server applications

ch05.title: Observability & Guardrails
ch05.body:
## Observability & Guardrails

An agent in production is a system that makes real decisions with real consequences. Without visibility into what it is doing and constraints on what it is allowed to do, you are flying blind.

Observability tells you what happened. Guardrails control what can happen. Both are non-negotiable for production agents.

### Why Agents Are Hard to Observe

A traditional HTTP endpoint is simple to instrument: one request in, one response out. An agent is a loop of variable length, calling tools in an order determined at runtime, making decisions you did not prescribe. A single user request might produce 12 API calls, 8 tool invocations, and 3,000 tokens of reasoning.

If something goes wrong, you need to answer: *Which turn did the agent go off track? What tool result confused it? How long did each step take? How much did this session cost?*

You cannot answer those questions without structured telemetry from inside the loop.

### Structured Logging

Every tool call should be logged with enough context to reconstruct the agent's journey:

```python
import logging
import json
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent
from claude_agent_sdk.callbacks import LoopCallback

log = logging.getLogger("agent.trace")

class TraceCallback(LoopCallback):
    def on_turn_start(self, turn: int, context: dict):
        log.info(json.dumps({"event": "turn_start", "turn": turn, "session": context["session_id"]}))

    def on_tool_call(self, tool_name: str, args: dict, session_id: str):
        log.info(json.dumps({"event": "tool_call", "tool": tool_name, "args": args, "session": session_id}))

    def on_tool_result(self, tool_name: str, result: dict, latency_ms: float, session_id: str):
        log.info(json.dumps({
            "event": "tool_result",
            "tool": tool_name,
            "success": "error" not in result,
            "latency_ms": latency_ms,
            "result_size": len(str(result)),
            "session": session_id,
        }))

    def on_loop_end(self, result, session_id: str):
        log.info(json.dumps({
            "event": "loop_end",
            "turns": result.iterations,
            "tool_calls": len(result.tool_calls),
            "input_tokens": result.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": result.usage.output_tokens,
            "session": session_id,
        }))

agent = Agent(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    callbacks=[TraceCallback()],
)
```

This produces a stream of JSON log lines for every session — indexable, queryable, and useful in post-mortems.

### Five Essential Guardrails

**1. Iteration limit** — Prevents infinite loops. Always set this.

```python
agent = Agent(model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_iterations=20)
```

**2. Token budget** — Prevents runaway cost. A single confused agent session should not consume $10 in API fees.

```python
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent, Guardrail

guardrails = [
    Guardrail.max_tool_calls(25),
    Guardrail.max_tokens(50_000),   # max total tokens per session
]
```

**3. Action confirmation** — High-consequence actions (send email, delete record, transfer funds) require explicit human confirmation before execution.

```python
guardrails.append(
    Guardrail.require_confirmation_for(["send_email", "delete_record", "submit_form"])
)
```

**4. Domain restriction** — Prevent the agent from fetching arbitrary URLs or calling arbitrary services.

```python
guardrails.append(
    Guardrail.allow_domains(["company.com", "trusted-api.example.com"])
    # or
    Guardrail.block_domains(["known-malware.example.com"])
)
```

**5. Output filtering** — Prevent sensitive data (API keys, PII, internal system details) from appearing in final responses.

```python
guardrails.append(
    Guardrail.redact_patterns([
        r'sk-ant-[a-zA-Z0-9]+',     # Anthropic API keys
        r'\b\d{16}\b',              # Credit card numbers
    ])
)
```

### Distributed Tracing with Langfuse

For multi-agent systems or production monitoring dashboards, integrate with Langfuse or Anthropic Console for distributed tracing:

```python
from langfuse import Langfuse
from claude_agent_sdk.integrations import LangfuseCallback

langfuse = Langfuse(public_key=os.environ["LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY"], secret_key=os.environ["LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY"])

agent = Agent(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    callbacks=[LangfuseCallback(langfuse)],
)
```

This gives you a visual timeline of every session: which tools were called, how long each took, where errors occurred, and aggregate metrics across all users.

### Incident Response Playbook

When something goes wrong, the investigation follows the trace:

1. **Find the session ID** from the user report or error log
2. **Pull the full trace** for that session ID
3. **Identify the turning point** — which turn produced the first wrong decision?
4. **Check the tool result** at that turn — did the tool return bad data?
5. **Check the model's reasoning** — did it misinterpret good data?
6. **Write a regression test** that reproduces the failure before fixing it

Observability is not just for debugging. It is for building confidence: when you can see exactly what your agent does in every session, you can deploy and improve it without fear.

> **Key insight:** Guardrails are not a sign of distrust in the model — they are sound engineering. Every complex system has circuit breakers. An agent without guardrails is an agent you cannot operate safely at scale.

---

### Further Reading

- [Claude Safety & Guardrails — Anthropic](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/guardrails) — Official guidance on input/output filters, content policies, and behavioral constraints for Claude agents
- [Langfuse Tracing Documentation](https://langfuse.com/docs/tracing) — How to implement distributed tracing for multi-turn agent sessions with dashboards and alerts
- [Structured Logging in Python](https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html) — Python's logging module with JSON formatting for the structured telemetry patterns shown in this lesson
- [OWASP LLM Top 10](https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/) — The ten most critical security risks for LLM-powered applications — required reading before production deployment
- [Anthropic Usage Policies](https://www.anthropic.com/usage-policy) — What Claude can and cannot be used for — foundational reading before deploying any production agent

ch06.title: Orchestrators and Workers
ch06.body:
## Orchestrators and Workers

### The Limits of a Single Agent

A well-built single agent can handle a surprising range of tasks. But as you push agents into harder territory — longer workflows, more tools, more complex reasoning — you run into a ceiling that is not about the model's intelligence. It is about *cognitive load*.

An agent that has 20 tools, a 10,000-token system prompt combining 7 different skills, and a conversation history that spans 40 turns is doing too many things in a single context. The model's attention is stretched thin. Tool selection degrades. Important context from earlier in the session gets pushed out. The longer the session runs, the worse the performance gets.

This is the core problem that multi-agent architecture solves. Instead of one agent doing everything, you have a **network of specialised agents** — each narrowly focused, each operating in a clean context — coordinated by an orchestrator that routes work to the right worker at the right time.

### The Orchestrator-Worker Pattern

The most common multi-agent architecture has two roles:

**Orchestrator** — The coordinator. It receives the user's goal, breaks it into subtasks, delegates each subtask to the appropriate worker agent, collects and synthesises the results, and delivers a final response. The orchestrator does not do domain work itself — it manages the process.

**Worker** — A specialist. Each worker is a narrowly focused agent with deep capability in one domain: web research, code execution, data analysis, email drafting. Workers receive a specific subtask, complete it, and return a result. They do not know about the larger goal.

```
User goal: "Research our top 3 competitors, analyse their pricing, and draft a strategy memo."

Orchestrator
├── → ResearchWorker: "Find and summarise competitor A's product and pricing"
├── → ResearchWorker: "Find and summarise competitor B's product and pricing"
├── → ResearchWorker: "Find and summarise competitor C's product and pricing"
├── → AnalysisWorker: "Compare the three pricing structures and identify our positioning"
└── → WritingWorker: "Draft a 1-page strategy memo based on this analysis"
```

### Why Specialisation Works

A worker agent that only does web research has:
- A short, focused system prompt with clear research instructions
- Three or four research-specific tools (no unrelated capabilities)
- Clean context — only the current subtask, not the entire conversation history
- A well-defined output format the orchestrator can reliably parse

This is dramatically more reliable than one giant agent trying to switch modes between researching, analysing, and writing within the same context.

### When to Use Multi-Agent vs. Single-Agent

| Scenario | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| Task fits in ~15 tool calls | Single agent — less overhead |
| Task requires 3+ distinct domains | Multi-agent — specialise workers |
| Subtasks can run independently | Multi-agent — parallelise |
| Task requires sequential handoffs | Multi-agent — chain workers |
| You need fine-grained audit trails | Multi-agent — each worker's trace is isolated |
| Low latency is critical | Single agent — no orchestration overhead |

### The Trust Boundary

In multi-agent systems, the orchestrator's tool calls are instructions to worker agents. This creates a trust boundary worth understanding: a worker that blindly executes any instruction from any orchestrator is a security risk. In production systems, worker agents should validate that instructions are within their sanctioned scope, just as they would validate user input.

We will cover this in depth in Chapter 8. For now, the key principle: **treat orchestrator-to-worker communication with the same caution as user-to-agent communication.**

> **Key insight:** The goal of multi-agent architecture is not more power — it is more reliability. Smaller, focused agents consistently outperform one large, overloaded agent on complex tasks. The orchestrator's job is to be a great manager: delegate clearly, synthesise well, and stay out of the way.

---

### Further Reading

- [Multi-Agent Orchestration — Anthropic Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/multi-agent-orchestration) — Official guide to building orchestrator-worker systems with Claude
